blurb: Uzbek has no “to have” — things simply exist

sections.heading: There is no “to have”
body: Uzbek has no verb “to have”. Instead, you say a thing **exists**: **bor** (“there is”) — or doesn't: **yo'q** (“there is not”).
**Mening kitobim bor** — “my book exists” — is how you say *I have a book*. **Vaqtim yo'q** — “my time doesn't exist” — *I have no time*.

examples.english: I have a book (lit. “my book exists”)

sections.heading: bor and yo'q run the bazaar
body: These two little words are everywhere. Add the question particle **-mi** and you can ask for anything: **Choy bormi?** — is there tea? **Bor!** — there is! **Yo'q** — there isn't.
Ask it at a choyxona, a shop, a ticket office — bormi? works for everything.

examples.english: Is there tea? / Do you have tea?
Is there bread? / Do you have bread?
Are there tickets? / Do you have tickets?

exercises.translation: I have a book — literally “my book exists”.

options.explain: My time doesn't exist — yo'q negates existence.
That means you DO have time — bor asserts existence.
emas negates descriptions (“is not”), not existence — that says “I am not time”!

exercises.instruction: You ask “Choy bormi?” and the choyxona owner beams: “Bor!” That means…

options.explain: Bor! — it exists. Sit down, the tea is coming.
bor only ever means “it exists” — good news for you.

wrapUp: Things exist (bor) or they don't (yo'q) — that's how Uzbek “has” anything. Choy bormi? Bor!
